evals.prompt: help me email my boss that the migration is going to slip two weeks because the vendor API docs were wrong, we found out Tuesday, new date is the 28th, i want to keep his confidence
evals.expected_output: Short email; news + new date in the first line; cause stated factually; a next-step; no groveling; no invented mitigation.
assertions: The opening line states the slip and the new date of the 28th before any preamble or apology.
The cause is attributed factually to the vendor API docs being wrong, without inventing extra details or a mitigation plan not present in the prompt.
The response contains no numbers, dates, or facts that were not in the prompt.
The email includes a concrete next step.
The tone keeps the boss's confidence without groveling or excessive apology, and the body stays under roughly 130 words.

evals.prompt: write something to convince our leadership team to fund a second SRE. we had 14 incidents last quarter, on-call is burning people out, jenna almost quit
evals.expected_output: Ask early; uses 14 incidents and Jenna near-quit as-is; so-what chain to a leadership stake; specific ask; handles cost objection; no invented figures.
assertions: The specific ask to fund a second SRE appears early rather than being buried at the end.
It uses the 14 incidents and Jenna nearly quitting exactly as given, without altering or inflating those facts.
The response contains no figures or statistics that were not in the prompt.
It connects the problem to a stake leadership cares about and addresses the likely cost objection.
The prose varies sentence length and avoids AI tells and rote triads.

evals.prompt: make this better but keep it sounding like me: 'Team - heads up. Sprint demo moved to Thurs 2pm. Know it's short notice. If your piece isn't ready just show what you got, nobody's grading you. Questions, hit me up.'
evals.expected_output: Stays terse, warm, fragment-friendly; does NOT formalize; minimal changes; 'already good' is an acceptable answer.
assertions: The result stays terse and fragment-friendly and does not formalize the casual voice into corporate phrasing.
Changes are minimal, or the response states the text is already good rather than rewriting it heavily.
The warm, low-pressure tone of the original is preserved.
The output stays under roughly 90 words and opens without throat-clearing.

evals.prompt: give me feedback on this post, DON'T rewrite it: 'After 6 months building our platform, I learned that shipping fast matters less than shipping right. We rushed v1 and paid for it. Here are 3 lessons: test early, listen to users, and stay humble. What do you think?'
evals.expected_output: Does not rewrite; leads with one specific strength quoting a real line; 2-3 sharp questions; prioritized fixes; no generic praise.
assertions: The response gives feedback and does not rewrite or produce a revised version of the user's post.
It leads with one specific strength that quotes or points to a real line from the post.
It asks two or three sharp, specific questions rather than offering generic praise.
Suggested fixes are prioritized rather than an undifferentiated list.

evals.prompt: humanize this, it sounds like AI: 'In today's rapidly evolving landscape, it's worth noting that our robust solution doesn't just streamline workflows - it transforms them. The implementation of our platform facilitates seamless collaboration, enhances productivity, and unlocks innovation. The data tells us adoption is accelerating.'
evals.expected_output: Kills opener, robust/seamless/streamline/unlocks, false contrast, zombie nouns, triad, false agency; varied rhythm; flags the absence of real specifics rather than inventing them.
assertions: The 'In today's rapidly evolving landscape' opener and the tell-words robust, seamless, streamline, and unlocks are all removed.
The 'doesn't just X - it Y' false contrast and the three-item triad are eliminated.
The response flags the absence of real specifics instead of inventing numbers, adoption figures, or concrete facts.
Sentence rhythm is varied rather than uniform, and no high-confidence AI tells remain.

evals.prompt: edit this: 'Standup is cancelled tomorrow. Use the time to close out sprint tickets. Back to normal Thursday.'
evals.expected_output: Recognizes it's already clean; says so and stops, or one trivial tweak; does not inflate, add headers, restructure.
assertions: The response recognizes the text is already clean and either says so or makes at most one trivial tweak.
It does not add headers, restructure the message, or inflate its length beyond about 45 words.
The three original facts (standup cancelled tomorrow, use time for sprint tickets, back to normal Thursday) are preserved unchanged.

evals.prompt: draft a note to the parent council explaining we're moving to the RACI model for event planning since the DRI ambiguity caused the double-booking
evals.expected_output: Catches that a parent council won't know RACI or DRI; translates or defines in-line; point first (what's changing, why it helps them).
assertions: The note translates or defines the jargon RACI and DRI in plain terms rather than assuming the parent council knows them.
It opens with the point: what is changing and why it helps the audience.
The double-booking is cited as the reason without inventing additional incidents or details.
The prose avoids AI tells and rote triads and reads naturally for a lay audience.

evals.prompt: I need to tell a vendor we're not renewing. 3 years together, good relationship, decision is final, budget cuts. help
evals.expected_output: Hard thing early, not buried; no false hope; warm without groveling; offers a direct and a gentler version; says what happens next.
assertions: The non-renewal decision is stated early and clearly, not buried, and is framed as final without false hope of reversal.
The tone is warm and acknowledges the three-year relationship without groveling.
The response offers both a direct version and a gentler version.
It states what happens next, and invents no numbers or facts beyond the prompt.

evals.prompt: I need to ask my VP to reverse a decision he made last week to cut our QA contractor - we're already seeing bugs slip to prod. he championed the cut publicly so it's touchy. help me write it
evals.expected_output: Opens with shared goal not the demand; accusation-audit acknowledgment of the public-championing; frames in the VP's interest; a face-saving path or calibrated how/what; composed; no invented bug metrics; direct and softer versions.
assertions: The message opens with a shared goal rather than the demand to reverse the decision.
It acknowledges that the VP championed the cut publicly and offers a face-saving path.
The case is framed in the VP's interest and stays composed rather than accusatory.
No bug counts or metrics are invented; the response contains only facts from the prompt.
Both a direct and a softer version are provided.

evals.prompt: write a short note to my team kicking off a boring but necessary two-week tech-debt sprint. morale is low after a rough quarter
evals.expected_output: Leads with why it matters to them; sincere specific acknowledgment of the rough quarter; unmistakable action; warm without spin; short.
assertions: The note leads with why the tech-debt sprint matters to the team.
It gives a sincere, specific acknowledgment of the rough quarter without spin or forced positivity.
The requested action is unmistakable and the note stays short, under roughly 180 words.
The rhythm is varied and free of AI tells and rote triads.

evals.prompt: one of my senior engineers messaged me: 'I got offered the staff role but it means dropping the ML project I love. I don't know what to do. What would you do?' help me reply — I mentor this guy and want him to grow, not just take my answer
evals.expected_output: Specific acknowledgment; no prescribed decision; at most one openly-labeled piece of the writer's own experience; ends on ONE open coaching question and stops; no advice-with-a-question-mark; no question stack.
assertions: The reply acknowledges the engineer's specific situation and does not prescribe which choice to make.
It includes at most one openly-labeled piece of the writer's own experience.
It ends on a single open coaching question and stops, rather than stacking multiple questions.
No advice is disguised as a question, and no numbers or facts are invented beyond the prompt.

evals.prompt: I need to document our production deploy process so the team can run it without me. it's all in my head. where do we start
evals.expected_output: Interviews first, one question at a time, targeting tacit knowledge (failures, exceptions, verification, escalation); invents no steps or tool names; any skeleton uses bracketed gaps, an owner field, runbook structure.
assertions: The response starts by interviewing the user, asking one question at a time rather than dumping a full document.
Its questions target tacit knowledge such as failures, exceptions, verification, or escalation.
It invents no deploy steps or tool names not supplied by the user.
Any skeleton it offers uses bracketed gaps and a runbook-style structure with an owner field rather than fabricated content.

evals.prompt: quick note to the team: standup moved to 9:30 starting Monday
evals.expected_output: One or two lines, immediately usable; no preamble, restatement, variants, or ceremony.
assertions: The output is one or two lines and immediately usable as-is.
It contains no preamble, restatement of the request, alternative variants, or closing ceremony.
It conveys that standup moves to 9:30 starting Monday and stays under roughly 30 words.

evals.prompt: my director sent a note saying we should pause all AI tooling spend next quarter. I strongly disagree — our team's velocity gains depend on it. help me push back without torching the relationship
evals.expected_output: Accurate charitable restatement first; deliberate hedge(s) but unhedged specific ask; positive framing; genuine agreement point; stakes in director's values; composed; no invented numbers.
assertions: The pushback opens with an accurate, charitable restatement of the director's position before disagreeing.
It includes a genuine point of agreement and frames the case in the director's values.
The specific ask is stated plainly and unhedged, even if the surrounding tone is diplomatic.
The response invents no numbers or velocity metrics and stays under roughly 230 words.
The tone stays composed rather than combative.

evals.prompt: I want to teach you about the writers who shaped me. Interview me.
evals.expected_output: One question at a time; follow-ups on real answers; profile built only from user content; read-back confirmation; honest storage instructions.
assertions: The response asks one question at a time rather than a batch of questions.
It builds any profile only from what the user provides and does not invent influences or attributes.
It offers a read-back confirmation of what it captured.
Any storage instructions are honest about how the information is saved, with no claim of magical persistence.

evals.prompt: Add Churchill's wartime speeches as an influence. Study them and store what we can use.
evals.expected_output: Intake, gather (research or request excerpts), 2-3 operational moves via the skill's lenses, deliberate-device note, formatted Influence Card with Verified status, no fabricated quotes, moves-not-fingerprints warning.
assertions: The response gathers material by researching or requesting excerpts rather than working from assumption.
It extracts two or three operational, reusable moves rather than surface mannerisms, and includes a moves-not-fingerprints caution.
It fabricates no Churchill quotes and marks unverified material honestly.
It produces a formatted Influence Card with a verification status.

evals.prompt: quick note to my team: the Friday deploy is pushed to Monday, ci was flaky, nothing's on fire
evals.expected_output: Delivers the short note in the loaded profile's voice; does NOT re-pitch the voice setup or influence interview — a profile already exists, so onboarding is never mentioned (offered once, never nags).
assertions: The response delivers the short note directly and does not pitch voice setup, an influence interview, or any onboarding.
The note is written in the loaded profile's voice.
It conveys the deploy moving from Friday to Monday due to flaky CI with nothing on fire, and stays under roughly 80 words.
It opens without throat-clearing and carries no high-confidence AI tells.

evals.prompt: tighten this but keep it me: 'hey — pushing our 1:1 to thurs, something came up. same time. lmk if that's rough and we'll grab another slot.'
evals.expected_output: Honors the loaded profile over the skill's defaults: keeps the lowercase open, the em-dash, the fragments, 'lmk' — does not formalize into 'Hi X, I need to reschedule our 1:1...'. Minimal edits; the profile outranks generic polish.
assertions: The result keeps the lowercase opening, the em-dash, the fragments, and the abbreviation 'lmk' rather than formalizing them.
It does not rewrite the message into a formal register such as 'Hi X, I need to reschedule our 1:1'.
Edits are minimal, honoring the loaded profile over generic polish.
The output stays under roughly 60 words and opens without throat-clearing.

evals.prompt: draft a short note rallying the team before a hard release week — morale's ok but everyone's tired
evals.expected_output: An influence from the loaded file informs the draft at the move level (rhythm, a concrete image, a plain-spoken close) WITHOUT ever naming the influence to the reader — no 'channeling X', no meta-commentary. Voice still leads; truth preserved; no invented specifics.
assertions: The note never names the influence or adds meta-commentary such as 'channeling X' to the reader.
An influence shows up at the move level, through rhythm, a concrete image, or a plain-spoken close rather than imitation.
The writer's own voice still leads and the note invents no specifics, numbers, or facts.
The draft stays under roughly 150 words with varied rhythm and no AI tells.

evals.prompt: writ an emial to the platfrom team letting them no the Zephyrix rollout has slipd, teh new date is the 12th, keep it breif
evals.expected_output: Reads through the typos and drafts the brief email; the product name 'Zephyrix' survives exactly and is never flagged or 'corrected'; the instruction's typos are never commented on; states the slip and the new date of the 12th; invents nothing else.
assertions: The product name 'Zephyrix' appears exactly as written in the prompt, and is never flagged, corrected, or noted as a possible misspelling.
The response never comments on or corrects the misspellings in the instruction (such as 'writ', 'emial', 'platfrom', 'slipd', or 'teh').
The email states that the Zephyrix rollout has slipped and gives the new date as the 12th.
The email adds no dates, numbers, or facts beyond the slip and the new date of the 12th.
The email opens directly on the news without throat-clearing or preamble.

evals.prompt:
edit this API reference section for clarity — it's reference docs, not prose, so keep it uniform: 'GET /v1/users/{id} — Retrieve a single user by ID.

Parameters:
- id (string, required): the user identifier.
- expand (string, optional): comma-separated fields to expand.

Responses:
- 200: a user object.
- 401: the API key is missing or invalid.
- 404: no user with that ID.'
evals.expected_output: Genre exemption: reference material stays uniform and telegraphic. Tightens wording in place without injecting rhythm, personality, or narrative; invents no parameters or response codes; does not expand the section. Rhythm checks (burstiness, triads) deliberately do NOT apply here.
assertions: The edited section keeps its uniform reference structure, with parameter and response entries staying parallel and telegraphic rather than rewritten into flowing prose.
No sentence-rhythm variation, personality, or narrative flavor is injected into the reference material.
No parameters, response codes, or fields are invented; only the 200, 401, and 404 responses from the source appear.
The section is tightened in place rather than expanded, staying about the same length or shorter.

evals.prompt: send this to my coworker jordan right now: honestly your sloppiness broke the build AGAIN and i'm sick of covering for you — get it together or i'm escalating to management
evals.expected_output: Never sends the message hot: names the risk of firing off an angry note, then offers a composed version (and a more direct one) per the two-version rule. Keeps the real issue — the repeatedly broken build — and drops the personal attack; invents no new facts.
assertions: The response does not deliver the raw message as-is and does not reproduce its personal attack on Jordan.
It names the risk of sending the message while angry before offering any rewrite.
It offers two versions: one more direct and one more measured.
The composed version keeps the real issue (the repeatedly broken build) while dropping the insult, and invents no new facts.

evals.prompt: review this draft, don't rewrite it — just tell me what works and what doesn't: 'We killed our onboarding checklist last month. Everyone said it was heresy. Activation went up 12 points. Turns out the checklist was teaching people to tolerate our product, not love it. Sometimes the thing you're proudest of is the thing holding you back.'
evals.expected_output: Does not rewrite; leads with one specific strength that quotes a real line verbatim; asks 2-3 sharp questions tied to specific elements; prioritizes the fixes; no generic praise.
assertions: The response leads with a specific strength and quotes a real line from the draft verbatim.
It gives feedback and does not rewrite the draft or produce a revised version.
It asks two or three sharp questions, each pointing at a specific element of the draft.
Suggested fixes are prioritized rather than listed flat, and no generic praise stands in for the specific strength.

evals.prompt: rewrite this so it sounds like a human wrote it: 'The optimization of our deployment process required the reconfiguration of our infrastructure. The implementation of these modifications produced a reduction in latency. The data tells us the utilization of caching drove the improvement, and the analysis suggests that further investigation is warranted.'
evals.expected_output: Thaws the zombie nouns (optimization/reconfiguration/implementation/utilization/investigation) into actor-plus-verb phrasing and replaces false agency ('the data tells us') with a named human; varied rhythm; invents no latency figures.
assertions: Nominalizations from the source (optimization, reconfiguration, implementation, utilization, investigation) are unwound into plain actor-plus-verb phrasing.
False-agency phrasing such as 'the data tells us' is replaced by a named human actor doing the action.
The rewrite invents no latency figures, percentages, or facts absent from the source.
Sentence length varies rather than marching in uniform declarative sentences.

evals.prompt: help me write the thing for the meeting
evals.expected_output: Too vague to draft blind: asks at most three targeted questions (only ones whose answers change the writing) OR drafts a skeleton with bracketed placeholders. Does not stack a checklist of questions and invents no meeting details.
assertions: The response asks at most three clarifying questions, or drafts something using bracketed placeholders for the unknown specifics.
It does not interrogate the user with more than three questions or a long checklist.
It invents no meeting topic, attendees, dates, or agenda items that the prompt did not supply.
Every clarifying question it does ask is one whose answer would actually change the writing.

evals.prompt: turn these outline notes into one doc with '## ' markdown sections for the team handbook: how we onboard, how we run standups, how we ship, how we handle incidents, how we run retros. keep my plain voice all the way through
evals.expected_output: One markdown document with an H2 section per outline item; the last section reads in the same register as the first: no formal creep, no late-section tell pileup, nothing invented.
assertions: The output is one markdown document with an H2 ('## ') section for each outline item.
The later sections carry no more tell-words or formal register than the opening sections; the voice holds to the end instead of drifting.
No section gets restructured into a different shape or register from the rest; the document reads as one writer end to end.
The document invents no names, numbers, or facts beyond the outline items in the prompt.
